question: How do I join the Office Hours or live workshop stream if I’m a student, and where is the video link usually posted?
answer_agent:
If you’re a student, you don’t join the Zoom directly. The **zoom link is only published to instructors/presenters/TAs**.

For students:
- **Watch on YouTube Live**
- **Ask questions via Slido** — the link is usually **pinned in the chat when the session is live**
- The **video link is usually posted in the announcements channel on Telegram and Slack before the session starts**
- You can also watch on the **DataTalksClub YouTube channel**

Also, don’t rely on posting questions in chat, since they can be missed.

The zoom link is only published to instructors/presenters/TAs.

Students participate via YouTube Live and submit questions to Slido (link is pinned in the chat when live). You can also watch live on the DataTalksClub [YouTube Channel](https://www.youtube.com/c/DataTalksClub).

Don’t post questions in chat as they may be missed if the room is very active.

question: Where should I begin if I’m just joining the LLM Zoomcamp, and what pages or repo should I check first?
answer_agent:
If you’re just joining LLM Zoomcamp, the best place to start is:

1. **LLM Zoomcamp docs**  
   https://datatalks.club/docs/courses/llm-zoomcamp/

2. **LLM Zoomcamp GitHub repo**  
   https://github.com/DataTalksClub/llm-zoomcamp

3. **Course logistics docs**  
   https://datatalks.club/docs/courses/zoomcamp-logistics/

If you want the current syllabus, deadlines, homework, and progress tracking, check the **course management platform**:
https://courses.datatalks.club/llm-zoomcamp-2026/

A typical workflow is:
- Watch the lesson videos
- Work through the lesson notebooks/code
- Read the homework instructions in the repo
- Submit homework through the course platform before the deadline

So, if you want the quickest “first stop,” go to the **GitHub repo**, then the **docs**.

question: How can I find my own entry on the leaderboard if I can’t spot my name right away?
answer_agent:
Use the **“Jump to your record on the leaderboard”** link — that takes you directly to your entry.

If you want to see what name appears publicly, open **Edit Course Profile**. The **first field** is your leaderboard/display name, and the **second field** is the official name used on your certificate.
answer_orig:
When you set up your account, you are automatically assigned a random name, such as “Lucid Elbakyan.” Click on the "Jump to your record on the leaderboard" link to find your entry.

If you want to see what your Display name is, click on the "Edit Course Profile" button.

<{IMAGE:image_1}>

- **First field:** This is your nickname/displayed name. You can change it if you want to be known by your Slack username, GitHub username, or any other nickname of your choice. This is useful if you want to remain anonymous.
- **Second field:** Change this to your official name as in your identification documents—passport, national ID card, driver's license, etc. This is mandatory if you do not want "Lucid Elbakyan" on your certificate. This name will appear on your Certificate!
tool_calls: [{'name': 'search', 'arguments': '{"query":"find my own entry on the leaderboard can\'t spot my name right away"}'}]

question: Can I do this course on my own schedule and still receive a certificate at the end?
answer_agent:
No — for this course, you can only receive a certificate if you finish with a live cohort. Self-paced participation does **not** qualify for a certificate.

The reason is that certificate eligibility requires peer-reviewing capstones during the active course period, and that peer-review process is only available while the course is running.
answer_orig:
No, you can only get a certificate if you finish the course with a "live" cohort.

We don't award certificates for the self-paced mode. The reason is you need to peer-review 3 capstone(s) after submitting your project.

You can only peer-review projects at the time the course is running; after the form is closed and the peer-review list is compiled.
